speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to need to go to the bathroom, too. It's the unfortunate part of drinking. This is reel four of the interview with Henry Rosmarin. Henry, we were talking about the ghetto in Czeladz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you begin seeing changes in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The changes in the ghetto were becoming-- the first thing that became obvious-- people were beginning-- you would miss some people. You'd say wait a minute, I haven't seen so-- what happened to him or to her? Until-- well, this is-- and mind you-- I have to back up a little bit and tell you this is-- we sort of were cut off from the world. You're in a vacuum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's a storm or-- [NON-ENGLISH] in the eye of-- in the eye of a hurricane, you know? And it's almost like-- it's quiet and-- and-- but around you, everything is whirling and stormy, right? So that was in the ghetto, where all of a sudden, we-- we start missing people. And then we start hearing-- because you couldn't pick up a newspaper or listen to a radio and, say, you know, a soundbite. Or-- or even a radio. If they found a radio, they probably-- the punishment would be nothing short of, perhaps, getting shot against the wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they'd line you up against the wall because there would be a contraband, it would be communication with the enemy. Listening to false propaganda from-- from-- from the war mongers in other countries and spreading it inside-- inside the German territories. So therefore, news would only be word of mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And someone would say, you know who they were deporting? It's militia. Militia-- I've got to explain what the militia-- called [NON-ENGLISH]-- nothing to do with the law enforcement of-- or police or anything. This is ghetto police-- Jewish ghetto police. The way you would tell them apart was a cap-- a white cap-- a kind of tall cap, like a-- like a-- like a captain on a-- on a cruise-- some comparison. And he would have an arm band with the Jewish Star, of course, and then said, Ordnungspolizei. Ordnungspolizei. Ordnungs means order. Police of the order, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they-- so their function was-- other than do-- do the dirty work for the Nazis-- all of the work was done. They-- they did all of the dirty work. Also keep order. Keep things quiet. Even-- even when they-- when they march you to work. There was a uniform-- a German guard-- and two or three [NON-ENGLISH], which would be the ordners. Or we would call them ordners. They would stand by. They would march outside the formation, make sure everybody marched in step. And you know, so they would be the one who were assisting in the deportation. Assisting-- would be, perhaps, minimizing their work, I think. They were instrumental. They were the one who kept on rapping on the door or kicking the door in and kept saying, arouse. And people would-- and some of them would continue the German lies and say, don't worry. Don't make a big deal. You will pick up what you need. You're going to a better place-- a work camp and all of-- all of this-- all this propaganda that the Germans would hand out or they would just pass on. Whether they believe it or not themselves, I don't know. But even if they didn't, it was a means-- it was their job, so they said. And it was a means-- this provided a means of their own survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the changes that were beginning were quite vivid-- remarkable so that people were missing and at night you're here. And they would come in-- one time, I remember-- I'll never forget this guy. He was a decent ordner. His name was Miklice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wherever he is, may the one above show some kindness to him, at least. Because in a couple instances-- in our, at least-- he took a liking to us-- my father and my family. He warned us about an upcoming-- they called it a [NON-ENGLISH], which means a raid. They would raid at night. They'd knock on the door, pick up, and start deporting. But-- and he would say-- I'll never forget this moment. One night, he came up and says, you know, tonight, don't sleep. After dark, stay-- stay in the back yard. Go-- go outside. If you go across the Arya-- the Aryan side-- the Christian side-- go hide. Because they're-- they're going-- your part of the ghetto is being-- is being marked for deportation tonight. We're going to riad-- we're going to raid it. And it happened. We went to a-- to a garden across the wall-- to a Christian garden at night. They could-- if someone would catch us, they'd think we were stealing something. We just laid low between the tomato patch and the vegetables and we could hear, 50 yards away, they were kicking the doors-- arouse, arouse, arouse. And then, about an hour later, it was quiet down. We went back in and everything was fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we are really thankful to that guy. He saved us for-- for another couple months or so. But there was-- just unreasonable to-- there's always a human-- a human being somehow surfaces in-- in-- in-- in-- even in the worst of our human species.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were the radical, radical changes. Again, coming back to the factory. See, we had this-- it was called in those days [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] meaning special. We had special papers. Because the director of the factory kept telling the authorities that we are-- we are so dearly needed. They gave us special pass. We could go outside the ghetto in formation. We always marched in formation. You never-- you never went by yourself. And 40 people or 45-- whoever was missing-- you know, you'd be written up. But we'd come back the same amount. And we would come with these papers and, of course, then we could leave the ghetto and come back. And that enabled us to do, at least, postpone-- to stave off-- the deportation until the moments for another-- until 1943, which is almost four years into-- yeah, well, four years into the war. And not being deported. Even though most of the ghetto was already empty. But this guy saved us. This one particular place, where, as in Warsaw ghetto, was like Umshlagplatz, this one was a place of assembly. The Germans would-- they would know how to hurt the Jewish people, not only physically, but mentally, as well. For instance, they would-- the biggest raid was always on high holidays or Jewish holidays of Passover or Shavuot or Sukkot. They would also take institutions such as a Jewish orphanage-- which was a pre-war orphanage in this one town of [NON-ENGLISH], which was the adjoining town-- the next big ghetto. And they would make the place of assembly for deportation. This is where they would bring people in and separate women, children, sick, old, young. And from there, they would ship to Auschwitz, to deportation, to death camps, of course. And some to work camps. But the sierociniec-- that's a Polish name. Sierociniec means orphanage. The orphanage was the assembly place. And that was rather strange that-- that-- that the Jewish orphanage was picked for-- for that place to-- to-- to become a place where they would make orphans of all of us. I-- I would like to say, one little incident comes to mind. And one of the raids that they went through the ghetto, one of my close friends-- his name was Itzkovitch. They took his mother. His father died before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took his mother and they took his-- his sister. He had a brother and a sister. They took them months before. But that one night, they took his mother. And all of a sudden, we started missing-- his name was Itzkovitch. Where is Itzkovitch?

speaker: interviewee
text: And someone said, they deported him. And somebody said no, no. He was-- he was-- he was working the factory. He has the papers. They couldn't deport him. Until we found out, maybe two or three weeks later, that he was-- he was locked up in his-- in-- in his one room. After they took his mother, he wouldn't even come out or go to work. He just-- he started to hide out in the beginning. But then, he just gave up. They finally found him and deported him. He must have been starved or lost his mind. I didn't realize then, but now I know what happened. He had a nervous breakdown. And I was so moved by it that I wrote a song-- a Polish song in Polish. That's the only language I could speak well in those days. Both lyrics and music. And I still have it and sometimes I play it for our people. And in the ghetto, we used to sing that song. It's a very haunting melody and it's about-- it's about this boy who-- who-- whose mother was taken away from him and he had no one else left. And I just pictured his face in front of my eyes-- what that would be like. Because he was our closest friend. He was one of the five, six boys and we hung around together. And all of a sudden, we lost him and-- because what happened to his mother. And the strange part is, the lyrics of the song-- it's a-- it repeats the story about how the-- how the [NON-ENGLISH]-- how they hit on the door. They kicked the door in. They were grabbing people and they put them in this sierociniec-- in this orphanage. That's when they were separated. Six months later-- maybe even more than-- maybe eight months later-- this was in the fall when I-- when this happened to him in '42. Then in March or April of '43, it happened exactly to my mother-- what the song was all about. That's how my mother went.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was almost like writing something and then-- and then-- almost like a prediction, if you will, or premonition. And it actually happened. It happened to me also like that. And I had to deal with it, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was the ghetto-- the-- coming back to your original question-- the changes in the ghetto. These were really horrible deportations, when they began cleansing the ghettos. We thought maybe they would leave us alone in the ghetto. But it wasn't good enough for them. They had to-- they had to cleanse that territory from what they called judenrein. Juden means Jew. Rein means clean. Clean of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with your mother in April of '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're getting into-- into the painful part of-- of my recollections now. The director who-- who was-- worked in a factory-- and as I described him before, he was a mean looking guy, but yet he was a-- he was a human being, as it later turned out. Two or three times when they grabbed us in the ghetto, he would come in-- come to the police headquarters and argue with them and got us out. The last time-- the one before last-- yeah-- he-- yeah, they took us  in this sierociniec-- in this orphanage building. And they were shipping up-- shipping out in the morning. At dawn, this guy comes in. And as we were separated-- men separate, women separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, I see somebody standing there and gesticulating with his hands and arms and speaking with the-- with the SS people. And he kept saying, I got to have them with me. What do you mean? You know, really shouting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he would call out our names. He would see all of-- next thing I know, somebody takes a list and calls names and tell us, [NON-ENGLISH]. Out of formation, to the right. And he took us out again. And to-- and my-- so it's my brother, myself, and I. But our-- my mother was still to the left with the other women. And my brother did a-- did a heroic act. And this was-- you know, stepping up to an SS man, who has like a baseball bat in his hand, you know, who is hitting left and right for any miscue-- for any misstep, he would [NON-ENGLISH]. He would step up at-- and stand at attention. And he was fluent in German. He was very bright. He spoke good German. And he was kind of blonde and blue-eyed, as a matter of fact. And told them-- whatever he said-- that his mother is there, too. She works also in a factory. Actually, she didn't. But he-- he was just fibbing. She's needed, too, because she works in the kitchen. She cooks for the-- for the-- for-- for the workers. And the first thing that the SS man did is he slapped him left and right, which wasn't so bad because the one before that, he hit with his belt-like piece of wood and split-- I saw blood running out of the head. Two sisters were mauled together. And this-- this figure-- this picture has always been in front of my-- because they were screaming mother, mommy, mom. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And he kept hitting them. And you know-- and they went to another group. Went to the left. And he-- minutes later, my brother steps in front of this beast and tells him about my-- our mother, who was left in the group and she's needed in a-- in a factory because they feed us a meal once a day. Otherwise, we can't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, he slapped him left and right and told him you have a lot of guts, you stinking Jew. And the next thing he says-- which one is you-- which one is you filthy Jewess? He pointed, the one with the checkered coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said Mom, come on, come on, you know. And he pulled her out, miraculously. And she, too, was safe. They deported the rest of-- again, we were saved. And this German took us on an open truck-- took us to the factory where we worked. And my mother along with us. Except she didn't have a job in the  factory so we hid her. And the foreman knew so-- so at the end of the day, we went back to the ghetto and we went back to our little room. And she was safe for another six weeks. And the final time came-- again another raid. This time, the director came again and started arguing again. Except that this time it didn't help. He's separated all of us. They put us again in the sierociniec-- in this orphanage building. And from there, they were separated men, women. And-- no, I'm sorry. No, they asked us to march out. They said it was too much-- it was too many of us to be separated because they brought people from another ghetto. And they marched us to a place like a soccer field and we had to sit, squatting down. And I was with my mom and my-- and with my father. But my brother wasn't there. He-- he had the presence of mind. He grabbed somebody's band. Oh, he joined-- before that, in the ghetto, he joined a voluntary force of volunteer firefighters. He didn't have any equipment, but he had a red band that says Brandschutz. The German believes in titles. They believe in titles and authority. If you had a band or something written on a cap, they would-- you know-- they would-- they would respect it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they printed these bands. It was a red one with black letters, said Brandschutz. Brand means-- means fire, burning. Schutz means-- means-- means security. Schutz means to secure, to-- to-- to-- sort of a fire brigade, to use the equivalent in English. So he joined this fire brigade and he put this on and they gave him already like an authority. He could move around. So he would bring sodas and he'd bring water for the SS people. Not for us squatting down. But he could move around and he kept looking where-- where-- where is his family. So he finally found us and he told us, they-- they're deporting-- they're deporting us. He says, try to-- he says, you-- you-- you guys try to get to a work camp. And he says-- he said, Mom, you try to faint. I'll bring you out to another group. And she couldn't faint. She says, no, no, no, no. We'll be OK. So in the meantime, an SS  men saw him. He says, hey, you filthy-- where is the water? What's the matter with you? He says, oh, yeah. He says, coming. Coming, sir. That's the last time I saw him. I didn't see him anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're-- we're sitting there for another half an hour-- another hour. The sun is beating down by now. It's getting hot and we had parched lips. And-- and I would give anything for some water. All of a sudden, some more shouts. Men from 18 to 35 to the right. Stand up to the right. Women from the 16 to whatever. So my mom started pushing me. She says go, they're calling you. I said no, no, no, it's not me. She says, they calling for men from  18 to 35. I said, Mom, I don't want to go. She says go, go. There's no time. Go! You'll probably get-- it's like your brother said. He said it's going to be-- you go to a work camp. She said, I'll probably go with the women to kitchens. She said, just go. Listen to the orders. And I says, no, no. I want to go with you and I didn't want to go. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And besides, I'm not even 18. I said Mom, I'm not 18. She says, you are. You are 18. This was-- and it took less argument than what I'm telling you now. My story is perhaps longer than the whole argument took. The argument probably took seconds. I said no, I don't want to go. She said yes, you go and she sort of pushed me. When she pushed me out, I sort of half stand up and she pushed me. I fell down and I got up. And-- and before I know it, somebody else kicked me. And the SS man-- he grabbed by my neck and threw me over to the next bunch. And I-- and I kept looking back. I said, Mom. And I didn't see her anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm among a group of men and they ask us, you're going to march over to the other side of the field in five and five, you know. And I kept looking, where is-- where is Mom? And this group was taken away, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, they ask us again to sit down. And I hear people marching. Outside of the field was a small road, not wide enough for-- for-- for-- for even a horse and buggy. But just a foot-- a path, a path. And they're marching along the path and I see women and children marching. And all of a sudden, I say, well, then maybe Momma is among that group. And I-- you know, I looked in that group and I see women and children and they're-- and they-- they struggling and marching and carrying babies. And I-- and I kept looking and the sun was kind of against me. I put my hand in front of my-- my eyes. And I spotted this-- my momma wore a coat. She loved it very much. I remember she bought it shortly before the war. It was a blue-- a blue and white checkered coat. And she took that coat along. And she was wearing that coat and she had a kerchief over her head. And I spotted that checkered coat. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And I waved to her and she waved back. And said something-- I love you. [NON-ENGLISH]. She shouted back. I couldn't hear what she shouted back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's the last time I saw her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You know, the checkered coat-- that stand out in my mind. I never-- in my life, I never-- I never bought a piece of-- a jacket or a coat that would be blue and white checkered. I never would wear it. And you know, when I saw-- I'm getting ahead a little bit now, but there is a connection there. When I saw a couple years ago-- when it first came out four years ago. And I-- it's done in black and white. The genius of a director-- he had no way of knowing my story. I've never met the guy before. I'm talking about Steven Spielberg. God bless him. The film was done in black-- black and white. There was only one color there-- one piece of color. And it's the little girl who was wearing a red coat. I sat in that movie and I saw that red coat and I got choked up. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I thought about the checkered coat of my-- my mother. How I spotted her among hundreds of women. And I thought, what a genius of a-- of a movie maker-- how-- how to emphasize something. This is 50-some years and I remember the checkered coat. So there's-- there's a correlation, in some way, that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I thought maybe I would see her. Maybe she-- maybe she'd be in a camp and maybe we'd go to a camp, you know. And-- and-- and her last words were, try to save yourself. I remember her talking when we were-- she would say something like, is this possible, what they're doing to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: These two sisters-- they were from our town. This guy split their head open-- both of them. They-- all they-- all they were doing is-- they were-- they were going to push to-- to see to another group. But they turned around and says, Mom, Mom. They wanted to go back to their mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: That guy-- he says, you want to go to the mother? Sure. He split their head open. And my mother kept saying as we were still sitting, is this possible? Are they doing this to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I'll never forget it. She said, if-- if-- whoever will live through it, they will have to write-- make movies, write books, tell stories. And I'm so grateful to be able to tell this-- this story. And it was-- this was just like a prophecy. And-- and she kept saying, save yourself, save yourself. I said, Mom, you're going to be OK, too. She says, well, yes. But you're younger, you're younger. You-- save yourself. These are really prophetic statements-- prophetic words. And I-- I think about it many nights when I can't sleep. And if I see anything related to-- to-- to-- to a Holocaust story-- it may be a movie or I read a book or whatever. And I was separated from her. And my life-- if my life changed from the-- since-- at the moment they first bombed. That was [INAUDIBLE] on our time in [NON-ENGLISH]. My life changed 100% again from the time I was separated from my mom and dad. All of a sudden, I find myself on my own. Here, I'm 17 years old. You-- and with a bunch of strange people locked in a box car now. Towards the evening, they load us up in a box car. I didn't know where I was going, what's going to happen to us. And people who have-- [CLANKING SOUND] --who knew-- who knew the route-- they were-- like salespeople who traveled around. And they were looking out the little window on top from the box car. They'd say, oh, I think we passed so-and-so, [NON-ENGLISH] town. Oh, my God. [NON-ENGLISH]. That's on the way to Auschwitz-- to Auschwitzen. We already-- there were some rumors then-- then about Auschwitz. And somebody would say, shut up, don't spread doom here. We're not going to-- we're going to-- towards the Czech border. And all-- you know-- all this. And I'm sitting here-- and this is a dark box car. And you know, the-- and the train is moving and you hear the clickety-clack of the wheels. And my-- I was just hoping to get some water someplace because we were just-- it was a hot day in the sun. We were just laying there all day. And somebody began to cry and to-- and to pray.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and all of a sudden, I hear a voice that's familiar to me. And I-- and it's in the dark. And I hear-- and he-- and-- and somebody says, why are you pushing? I'm standing here. You're standing on my feet, you know. And-- and start an argument. And I recognize a voice familiar to me. It was my father's voice in the box car. And it was-- the argument was in Yiddish. And I-- and I-- and I heard this and I says, [NON-ENGLISH]. And he-- and he heard my voice and he says, [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, who is this? And we communicated. I says, come on, where are you? Keep talking. I'm pushing my way through in the dark. And I says, just follow your voice. And we reunited in that box car with my dad going to-- didn't know where.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was a trip to my first camp. It was a forced labor camp. We arrived in that camp about 5:00 or 6:00 in the morning. They opened the cars and they start unloading us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, let's take a pause for a moment while we change reel.
